# Introduction

The ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn Dynamics project is a
multi-year project led by Uu-a-thluk Fisheries, with
technical support from Landmark Fisheries Research. The
project has been focused on developing a Nuu-chah-nulth approach 
to ecosystem-based fisheries management (EBFM) for Pacific
Herring on the West Coast of Vancouver Island (WCVI).

This report summarises work during Phase 3 of the ?aayaaqa
(Herring) spawn dynamics project. The goal of this work is to 
evaluate potential re-opening procedures for the WCVI herring 
fishery in closed loop feedback simulations, also known as 
Management Strategy Evaluation. The evaluation is aimed at 
understanding whether re-openings can align with 
Nuu-chah-nulth goals and objectives, while also meeting
policy requirements of Fisheries and Oceans, Canada (DFO). 
While policy requires that re-opening procedures will perform well
at the stock assessment region (SAR) level, the benefits for 
Nuu-chah-nulth resource users and owners are at a more local scale 
relevant to their needs. Building on the Phase 2 recommendation, 
the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model is used as an operating model
(OM) within a closed-loop simulation framework to test
a potential WCVI herring re-opening procedure.

Earlier phases of the project laid the foundation for this work.
The first year of the project (Phase 1) focused on empirical analyses 
of data collected during herring spawn events. Those analyses showed 
that egg production per gram of body weight (fecundity) increases with 
age, and that older herring spawn earlier in the season [@lfr2024_NTC]. 
A conceptual simulation model was also developed to represent WCVI herring
population dynamics across nine Nuu-chah-nulth spawning
areas.

In the second year (Phase 2), modelling efforts expanded the
suite of WCVI herring models to include predation mortality,
spatial stock structure, and age-dependent egg production
[@lfr2025_NTC]. Two models were developed: a three-stock
model (*SISCAH-3S-Pred*) fit to data for Pacific Fishery
Management Areas (Stat Areas) 23, 24, and 25, and a nine-stock
simulation model (*SISCAH-9S-Pred*) calibrated from the
three-stock model. Phase 2 concluded that the three-stock
model is the most practical tool for MSE moving forward,
as the nine-stock model was largely insensitive to its
additional spatial and temporal detail, reflecting analyst
assumptions rather than inherent properties of the system.
The nine-stock model remains useful in an empirical
capacity for monitoring spawn dynamics at finer spatial
scales than the assessment model considers, similar to an 
exceptional circumstances framework.


## Management context

WCVI herring commercial fisheries have been closed since 2006.
A re-opening of the fishery would require a re-opening
procedure (RP) consistent with the DFO Precautionary
Approach framework [@dfo2009pa] that defines how stock
status is assessed and how a total allowable catch (TAC)
is determined [@dfo2024_herringIFMP]. Current BC
herring management procedures are developed and tested using
single-stock operating models that treat herring stock assessment
regions (SARs) as a panmictic population [@johnson2024SCAH; 
@DFO_2021a]. However, the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model from Phase 2 
suggests that the true population may be spatially structured 
and subject to higher natural mortality due to predation than is 
assumed by the standard density-dependent mortality (DDM) model 
used in DFO assessments.

<!-- TODO: Review MSE definition (Sean: tailor to NTC
herring context; clean up incomplete edits) -->
Management strategy evaluation (MSE) is a structured decision-making 
approach designed to measure the performance of fisheries management 
procedures against pre-determined objectives in the presence of 
uncertainty [@butterworth1999experiences; @punt2016management; 
@de-la-Mare1998Tidier-fisherie]. Objectives and their associated metrics are agreed upon
by managers, First Nations,
fishery stakeholders, and scientists. Operating models are then used to 
represent alternative states of nature and generate uncertain fishery 
dynamics and data. Procedures are tested in a feedback loop: data are 
generated by the OM, the assessment model estimates biomass, a harvest 
control rule (HCR) sets a total allowable catch (TAC) based on 
biomass estimates, and catch is removed from the simulated population
via fishing mortality. This process is repeated over a projection period 
and the resulting metrics for conservation and fishery objectives are 
used to rank performance.

A robust RP should perform acceptably not only under the
assumptions of the assessment model it is paired with,
but also under alternative hypotheses about population
dynamics. The alternative hypotheses here are represented
by the standard DFO *SISCAH-DDM* OM, and the *SISCAH-1S-Pred* 
and *SISCAH-3S-Pred* OMs developed as part of the NTC-BCSRIF 
project, which include explicit predation mortality and, 
for the latter, a finer spatial resolution of population dynamics. 
The finer resolution is a first step towards a framework that 
can test performance of RPs against fishery objectives that have 
been identified by the Nuu-chah-nulth, including but not limited 
to spawn presence/timing in specific bays, local harvest access, 
and persistence of spawn across years.

## Objectives

In this phase, a closed-loop simulation framework is
developed and used to test a proposed minimum escapement
harvest control rule for WCVI herring under three
operating models of increasing ecological complexity:

1. The *SISCAH-DDM* density-dependent mortality model, which
   represents the assumptions currently used in DFO stock
   assessments;

2. The *SISCAH-1S-Pred* single-stock model with explicit
   predation mortality [@doherty2024predation], which adds
   predation but retains a single-stock structure; and

3. The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* three-stock model with predation
   [@lfr2025_NTC], which adds both predation and spatial
   stock structure.

The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model serves as a robustness test
operating model. When the RP assumes a single panmictic
stock but the true population is spatially structured,
the MSE can evaluate how well the RP protects individual
Stat Area stocks and how spatial TAC distribution
methods affect outcomes. Three distribution methods
are tested for distributing the aggregate TAC among Stat 
Areas: (i) proportional to recent spawn indices, (ii) proportional 
to recent catches, and (iii) allocating the entire TAC to Stat 
Area 23 (Barkley Sound).
